images.caption: Figure 1:Overview of our study.Top:High-level model architecture. We train a single autoregressive model with next-token prediction for text and next-visual state prediction with flow matching.Bottom:We study five axes: visual representations, data, world modeling, architecture, and scaling.
Figure 2:Examples of training data.We train on diverse data including text, videos, image-text, and action-conditioned video.
Figure 3:Shared  Modality-specific FFN.Modality separation improves multimodal learning over shared FFN. We explore MoE design inSection˜6.1.

Figure 4:RAE outperforms VAEs for both generation and understanding.RAE (SigLIP 2) achieves the best performance on DPGBench, GenEval, and VQA while maintaining text perplexity comparable to the text-only baseline.
Figure 5:Visual data does not compete with text.Text + Video matches the text-only PPL on DCLM, suggesting that raw visual data is compatible with language modeling.
Figure 5:Visual data does not compete with text.Text + Video matches the text-only PPL on DCLM, suggesting that raw visual data is compatible with language modeling.
Figure 6:I/T data enables visual capabilities.I/T pairs are essential for visual understanding and generation. Text + Video contains no I/T data, so image generation is omitted.

Figure 7:I/T data sources exhibit complementary trade-offs.Each source of I/T data has distinct strengths across text perplexity, generation, and VQA. Combining MetaCLIP (I2T) with Shutterstock (T2I) captures the strengths of both, suggesting that strategically using different I/T sources can outperform using a single source of I/T data.

Figure 8:Multimodal co-training exceeds unimodal performance.Across all token budgets, adding text tokens to a fixed vision budget consistently improves generation quality (), surpassing the multimodal-only baseline ().

Figure 8:Multimodal co-training exceeds unimodal performance.Across all token budgets, adding text tokens to a fixed vision budget consistently improves generation quality (), surpassing the multimodal-only baseline ().

Figure 9:General pretraining outperforms specialized scaling.Supplementing 20B VQA tokens with general-purpose data (text, video, or image-text) yields higher accuracy than training on 100B VQA tokens alone.
Figure 10:Multimodal pretraining is better for VQA.Multimodal pretraining (dark bars) consistently outperforms text-only baselines (light bars) after finetuning on VQA.

header: 5Towards World Modeling in Unified Multimodal Models

images.caption: Figure 11:Navigation actions are encoded directly as text tokens.We utilize data from Navigation World Model (NWM), where a training sequence consists of four context frames and a navigation action formatted as natural language text. The model is trained to predict the resulting target view.
Figure 12:Unsupervised video data drives world modeling performance.Supplementing the NWM baseline with general-purpose video data yields the best performance.
Figure 12:Unsupervised video data drives world modeling performance.Supplementing the NWM baseline with general-purpose video data yields the best performance.
Figure 13:World modeling transfers with minimal alignment.Performance saturates at 1%, suggesting that the core capability is mostly acquired from general pretraining.

Figure 14:Zero-shot model controllability via natural language.Given four context frames (ctx0–ctx3), the model predicts eight future frames (p1-p8) conditioned on WASD-style or natural language navigation actions.

Figure 15:Fine-grained experts improve performance;xx-pred benefits high-dimensional diffusion.Increasing granularity improves all metrics up toG=16G{=}16.
For RAE (SigLIP 2),xx-pred (solid) outperformsvv-pred (dashed) on image generation; for VAE (FLUX.1),vv-pred is better.

Figure 16:Sparsity scales multimodal performance.At fixedactivecompute (16 experts), increasingtotalexpert count from 32 to 1008 (and thus total parameter count) consistently improves both language (lower PPL) and vision (lower diffusion loss, higher GenEval score).

Figure 17:Semantic representations scale better with sparsity.We fix 16 active experts and increase the total expert count. Text loss improves consistently regardless of the vision encoder, but diffusion loss saturates for VAE (FLUX.1) while continuing to improve for RAE (SigLIP 2).
Figure 18:Emergent Modality Specialization.Expert specialization forms naturally. Most experts are text-focused, but later layers contain more vision and multimodal experts.
Figure 18:Emergent Modality Specialization.Expert specialization forms naturally. Most experts are text-focused, but later layers contain more vision and multimodal experts.
Figure 19:Experts do not specialize in diffusion timesteps.CV scores are distributed near 0.15, indicating vision experts are time-invariant.

Figure 20:Visual generation and understanding share the same experts.We compare the expert selection rates for image generation (x-axis) versus image understanding (y-axis) across different network depths. We observe very high Pearson correlation (r=0.90\geq r=0.90) across all layers.

Figure 21:Modality-specific FFN, SigLIP 2, MoE, andxx-pred achieve best unified performance.Starting from a Transfusion baseline, we progressively stack the best design choices for FFN, vision encoder, capacity separation, and prediction target.
Each row showsPerplexity(\downarrow\downarrow)andDPG score(\uparrow\uparrow).

Figure 22:Semantic encoders and MoE capture more world knowledge.WISE scores across six knowledge categories and overall. Models with semantic encoders (MoE, MoT, SigLIP 2) consistently outperform VAE-based models (SD-VAE, FLUX.1) by 3–4, with MoE achieving the highest overall score.

Figure 23:Scaling laws for unified dense models.We compute IsoFLOP curves across compute budgets. We project the optimal model size and token count for each FLOP budget, and find that the vision modality is more data-hungry, while text follows Chinchilla-like scaling.

Figure 24:Scaling laws and efficiency benchmarks for unified dense models.We fitL​(C)=A​C−α+EL(C)=A\cdot C^{-\alpha}+Eto each model class. Dense Multimodal outperforms both the unimodal Text-Only and T2I-Only baselines across all four metrics.

Figure 25:Scaling laws for multimodal MoE models.MoE narrows the vision-language exponent gap from 0.10 (dense) to 0.05 (Dopt​C0.64D_{\text{opt}}\propto C^{0.64}for vision vs.C0.59C^{0.59}for language).

Figure 26:Scaling laws and efficiency benchmarks for unified MoE models.MoE Multimodal closely tracks unimodal MoE baselines across all four metrics, with near-identical FID and competitive language perplexity at102110^{21}FLOPs.
